title: Effects of Soil Type and Thermal Boundary on Predicting Temperature Profiles and Groundwater Fluxes

research.question: How do soil thermal properties and the lower subsurface thermal boundary affect analytical predictions of temperature-depth profiles and inverse estimates of vertical groundwater flux?
research.system: One-dimensional vertical heat conduction and groundwater advection through a homogeneous subsurface porous medium with time-varying surface temperature and alternative lower thermal boundary conditions.
research.inputData: Synthetic sand and clay thermal-property cases, six classical analytical solutions, prescribed surface-temperature histories, lower-boundary parameters, and measured Sendai Plain temperature-depth data digitized from a published observation-well profile.
research.method: Classify the six analytical solutions by state, domain, initial condition, and boundary condition; simulate synthetic profiles; compare boundary and soil effects; fit field boundary parameters; estimate vertical flux; and assess field agreement by RMSE.
research.benchmark: The finite-domain Chang and Tsai solution is compared with five steady-state or semi-infinite-domain solutions in synthetic cases and with three transient alternatives against the Sendai Plain field profile.
research.uncertainty: The study reports RMSE rather than a probabilistic uncertainty analysis. Interpretation is bounded by one-dimensional vertical transport, homogeneous media, steady groundwater flow, selected sand and clay properties, prescribed boundary forms, and one field application.
research.conclusion: Soil-type differences in transient temperature-depth profiles increase with time, semi-infinite solutions can miss deeper lower-boundary effects, and the finite-domain convective-boundary solution gives the smallest reported field RMSE with a fitted vertical flux of 0.33 m/year for the Sendai Plain case.

narrative.throughline: Follow a surface-temperature signal down a soil column and show how soil properties and the unseen lower boundary reshape the profile used to infer groundwater flux.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A borehole temperature-depth profile can reveal heat carried by vertical groundwater flow.
narrative.stakes: A model that assigns the wrong soil properties or removes the lower boundary can fit the wrong thermal path and return an inaccurate groundwater-flux estimate.
narrative.resolution: The six-solution comparison reconnects profile divergence and field-fit differences to the soil column and lower thermal boundary that generated them.

background.premise: Heat is treated as a groundwater tracer.
background.visibleEvidence: A temperature signal descends through a porous column beside a vertical groundwater-flow arrow.
background.whyNow: The tracer relation turns a borehole temperature profile into the concrete object whose interpretation is at stake.
background.boundary: The paper addresses vertical one-dimensional heat transport rather than a general multidimensional groundwater-flow reconstruction.
background.sourceExcerpt: Therefore, the heat is treated as a groundwater tracer
background.visualObject: Vertical porous column with a descending temperature signal and groundwater arrow
background.motionPurpose: Connect an observable temperature signal to otherwise difficult vertical groundwater movement
background.stepDetail: Pulse surface heat into the column and retain the groundwater arrow beside the evolving depth profile.
background.transitionBridge: Hold the profile in place while the bottom edge of the column becomes visible.

background.premise: A lower thermal boundary can alter transient TD profiles.
background.visibleEvidence: Two identical shallow profiles separate near a finite lower boundary while their surface histories remain matched.
background.whyNow: The lower edge explains why a semi-infinite transient model can diverge from a finite-domain profile at depth.
background.boundary: The effect concerns media whose underlying layer supplies a relevant thermal boundary within the modeled depth and time.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Research Methods, opening paragraph
background.sourceExcerpt: the lower thermal boundary effect on TD
background.visualObject: Paired finite and semi-infinite soil columns with matched surface forcing
background.motionPurpose: Reveal the hidden bottom boundary as a cause of deep profile separation
background.stepDetail: Align the surface signals, extend one column without a visible bottom, and let only the finite column receive lower-boundary heat.
background.transitionBridge: Keep both columns visible while their soil fills change from a common medium to sand and clay.

background.premise: Soil type may be an important factor in thermal prediction.
background.visibleEvidence: Sand and clay columns carry the same imposed surface signal through visibly distinct thermal-property labels.
background.whyNow: Soil-specific conductivity and heat capacity determine whether one parameter set can represent both profile responses.
background.boundary: The paper demonstrates the effect with common saturated sand and clay properties rather than an exhaustive soil catalog.
background.sourceExcerpt: Soil type may be an important factor
background.visualObject: Matched sand and clay columns with conductivity and heat-capacity markers
background.motionPurpose: Turn soil type from a label into a visible change in thermal response
background.stepDetail: Duplicate the bounded column, texture one as sand, texture one as clay, and attach the paper's property roles without adding unsupported values.
background.transitionBridge: Merge the two columns into the common conduction-advection equation used by the selected solutions.

methods.input: Subsurface temperature T over depth z and time t with thermal conductivity bulk heat capacity water heat capacity and vertical Darcy flux q
methods.output: Transient heat conduction-advection governing equation for the vertical porous medium
methods.validity: The formulation follows the paper's one-dimensional homogeneous-medium and steady-groundwater-flow assumptions.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Conceptual Model for Subsurface Heat Transfer, Figure 1 and Equation 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: the governing equation for the transient heat
methods.visualObject: Bounded soil column whose conductive spread and advective groundwater arrow become Equation 1 terms
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the visible heat paths into the shared mathematical model
methods.stepDetail: Retain the soil column, isolate the conductive depth curvature, isolate the advective flux arrow, and place the storage response on the opposite side.
methods.transitionBridge: Keep the governing relation fixed while six boundary-condition frames assemble around it.

methods.input: Six analytical heat-transfer solutions with their medium domains initial conditions top boundaries and lower boundaries
methods.output: Table 1 grouping into two steady-state finite-domain solutions and four transient solutions with distinct boundary structures
methods.validity: The categories reproduce the state domain initial-condition and boundary-condition distinctions listed in Table 1.
methods.sourceExcerpt: solutions are introduced next and categorized in Table 1
methods.visualObject: Six model frames sorted by steady or transient state and finite or semi-infinite depth
methods.motionPurpose: Expose which model structures retain time variation and which retain a lower boundary
methods.stepDetail: Place each named solution beside its domain, group the steady pair, group the transient quartet, and leave every lower-boundary icon visible.
methods.transitionBridge: Feed each classified frame the same synthetic parameter case.

methods.input: Selected analytical solution with input thermal parameter values for synthetic sand or clay cases
methods.output: Temperature-depth profiles for each specified solution soil case boundary setting and simulation time
methods.validity: The forward problem uses the selected solution with specified thermal parameters drawn from the paper's synthetic cases.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Research Methods, forward-problem paragraph; PDF pages 7-8, Figures 2-5
methods.sourceExcerpt: with the input of the thermal parameter values
methods.visualObject: One soil column generating source-defined temperature-depth traces under each selected solution
methods.motionPurpose: Show how a fixed model structure turns stated case inputs into a depth profile
methods.stepDetail: Attach the chosen parameter set to one classified model, sweep depth through the soil column, and reveal only the corresponding source-defined profile.
methods.transitionBridge: Align profiles that share initial and surface conditions for a lower-boundary comparison.

methods.input: Transient solution profiles with matched initial settings and their stated surface-temperature histories
methods.output: Depth-dependent deviation from CTS that becomes larger over the reported 25-year to 75-year comparison
methods.validity: Figure 4 compares TAS KMS KIS and finite-domain CTS under the paper's corresponding initial and top-boundary settings.
methods.locator: PDF pages 7-8, Effects of Subsurface Thermal Boundaries, Figures 3-4
methods.sourceExcerpt: the deviation of the curves from CTS becomes larger
methods.visualObject: Source-defined transient profiles sharing a shallow origin and separating near the lower boundary
methods.motionPurpose: Make increasing boundary-induced profile deviation legible along depth and reported time
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the source profiles at the reported times, preserve their common initial reference, and highlight only their separation from CTS at depth.
methods.transitionBridge: Keep the time markers while replacing the common soil with paired sand and clay media.

methods.input: Six solution profiles computed with the paper's saturated sand and clay thermal properties
methods.output: Profile disparity between different soil types that increases with time in each transient solution
methods.validity: Figure 5 uses the stated sand and clay conductivity and bulk heat-capacity values across all six selected solutions.
methods.locator: PDF pages 8-9, Effects of Soil Types on Predicting TD Profiles, Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: different soil types increases with time
methods.visualObject: Paired source-defined sand and clay profiles at the reported early and late times
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the soil-property effect from the lower-boundary comparison
methods.stepDetail: Hold each analytical solution fixed, overlay its sand and clay profiles at the reported times, and mark the source-described change in disparity.
methods.transitionBridge: Collapse the synthetic profiles into the measured Sendai borehole profile and its boundary observations.

methods.input: Measured surface-temperature variation curves for the Sendai field case
methods.output: Top-boundary parameters for each selected transient field solution
methods.validity: The paper obtains the top-boundary parameters by fitting the measured surface-temperature variation curves.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Research Methods, inverse-problem paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: parameters in the top BCs are obtained by fitting the measured data of surface temperature variation curves.
methods.visualObject: Measured Sendai surface-temperature history attached to the top of the soil column
methods.motionPurpose: Assign the top-boundary fit to the surface observation that constrains it
methods.stepDetail: Place the measured surface history at the top edge and fit only the corresponding top-boundary parameter handles.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted top boundary remains fixed while borehole temperatures constrain the lower boundary in M07.

methods.input: Measured borehole temperatures defining the lower thermal boundary
methods.output: Lower-boundary parameters for the finite-domain field solution
methods.validity: The paper obtains lower-boundary parameters by fitting measured borehole temperatures independently of the top-boundary fit.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Research Methods, inverse-problem paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: The parameters in the lower BCs are gained by fitting the measured temperature from boreholes.
methods.visualObject: Measured deepest-borehole temperature attached to the lower boundary of the finite soil column
methods.motionPurpose: Assign the lower-boundary fit to the borehole observation that constrains it
methods.stepDetail: Keep the fitted surface history visible, attach borehole temperature to the lower edge, and fit only the lower-boundary handles.
methods.transitionBridge: Both independently owned boundary fits remain locked while groundwater flux varies in M08.

methods.input: Each selected transient solution with known thermal parameters fitted boundary parameters and measured Sendai temperature-depth data
methods.output: Estimated vertical groundwater flux for each TAS KMS KIS and CTS field fit
methods.validity: The inverse procedure uses least-squares parameter estimation to fit measured temperature-depth profiles with known parameter values.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Research Methods, inverse-problem paragraph; PDF page 10, field method and Table 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: values are estimated using the selected
methods.visualObject: Groundwater-flux arrow adjusted beside each model profile until it meets the measured Sendai points
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal groundwater flux as the inferred quantity rather than a directly observed input
methods.stepDetail: Keep boundary parameters fixed, vary one vertical flux arrow per solution, and stop each source-defined profile at its reported field fit.
methods.transitionBridge: Retain the four fitted profiles and measured points for the RMSE calculation in M09.

methods.input: Observed Sendai temperature-depth data and four fitted transient solution profiles
methods.output: One root-mean-square error RMSE value for each fitted field solution
methods.validity: Table 2 reports RMSE between the observed data and the four fitted solutions.
methods.locator: PDF page 9, Estimation of Vertical Groundwater Fluxes from Field TD Data, Table 2
methods.visualObject: Residual segments from measured temperature points to each fitted profile collected into four RMSE markers
methods.motionPurpose: Turn visible point-to-profile mismatch into the paper's reported fit metric
methods.stepDetail: Draw residual segments at measured depths, gather the segments by solution, and reveal only the reported RMSE markers from Table 2.
methods.transitionBridge: Align the four owned RMSE markers beneath their corresponding profile overlays for comparison in M10.

methods.input: Four fitted field profiles with observed Sendai data and their reported RMSE values
methods.output: CTS identified as the best reported agreement with RMSE 0.0131 degrees Celsius and fitted q of 0.33 m/year
methods.validity: Figure 6 and Table 2 support the comparison for the digitized Well No. 4 profile under the stated field settings.
methods.locator: PDF pages 9-10, Estimation of Vertical Groundwater Fluxes from Field TD Data, Table 2 and Figure 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: reveals that the CTS agrees better with the observed
methods.visualObject: Measured Sendai profile with the four source-defined fitted profiles and CTS agreement emphasized
methods.motionPurpose: Connect model boundary structure to the reported field-fit ranking and flux estimate
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the four source profiles on the measured points, align each reported RMSE marker, and emphasize the CTS entry without inventing intermediate curves.
methods.transitionBridge: Return the emphasized fit to the finite soil column with its lower thermal boundary restored.

symbols.visualObject: Temperature field through the vertical porous column
symbols.meaning: Subsurface temperature at depth z and time t
symbols.transition: Color the column by temperature before attaching the T label

symbols.visualObject: Downward depth coordinate beside the soil column
symbols.transition: Extend the depth axis from the ground surface to the lower boundary

symbols.visualObject: Time marker attached to the evolving temperature profile
symbols.transition: Advance the time marker only after the initial profile is visible
Attach q to the groundwater arrow before it enters the advection term

symbols.visualObject: Conductive heat spread through the soil column
symbols.meaning: Thermal conductivity of the porous medium
symbols.transition: Color-match the conductivity label to the conductive spread

symbols.visualObject: Thermal storage fill within the porous medium
symbols.meaning: Bulk volumetric heat capacity of the medium
symbols.transition: Fill the soil column before mapping storage to the time-response term

symbols.visualObject: Bracket from the ground surface to the finite lower boundary
symbols.meaning: Depth of the subsurface medium boundary
symbols.transition: Grow the bracket when the finite-domain solutions are classified

symbols.visualObject: Heat-transfer marker at the lower convective boundary
symbols.meaning: Heat transfer coefficient at the lower boundary
symbols.transition: Attach the marker to the bottom interface after the boundary observation appears

symbols.visualObject: Grouped residual segments between observed points and a fitted profile
symbols.meaning: Root-mean-square temperature error for a field fit
symbols.transition: Gather visible residual segments into one fit-error marker

return.visualObject: Sendai borehole temperature-depth profile inside a finite alluvial-soil column with the surface history lower thermal boundary and fitted vertical flux arrow restored
return.claim: For the selected analytical solutions and cases the soil-type profile difference grows with time and ignoring a relevant lower thermal boundary can degrade field groundwater-flux estimation.
return.claimBoundary: The comparison is limited to one-dimensional homogeneous porous media with steady vertical groundwater flow the paper's sand and clay synthetic properties the selected boundary formulations and the digitized Sendai Plain field profile.
return.locator: PDF page 1, Abstract; PDF page 11, Conclusions, items 3-5
return.sourceExcerpt: but ignore possible thermal boundary effects in the subsurface
return.minimalText: Match soil and lower boundary to the site

visual.primaryObject: Vertical finite porous-medium column carrying a temperature-depth profile and groundwater-flux arrow
visual.accentObject: Lower thermal boundary switching between absent specific-temperature specific-heat-flux and convective representations
visual.measuredQuantity: Subsurface temperature versus depth with vertical groundwater flux inferred from field profile fitting
visual.geometryNotes: Begin with the Figure 1 vertical column. Preserve depth orientation while moving through the Table 1 model classes source-defined Figure 4 boundary comparisons Figure 5 sand-clay comparisons and Figure 6 Sendai field fits. Return to the finite column with the lower boundary and q arrow visible.
